CURRENT AFFAIRS,

After Ennis—Kilkenny. Mr. William Redmond is
getting well abused, and from experience we can say
that when you are being well abused the chances are
that you are doing your work effectwely, and making
solid friends as well as a few frothy enemies who don’t
count much. At the quarterly meeting of the Kilkenny
Corporation a Mr. Lenehan, almost needless to say, he
is a Bung, proposed :—* That we, the members of the
Kilkenny Corporation, desire to record our indignant
protest against the action of a member of the Irish Par-
liamentary Party, Mr. William Redmond, in alliance
with Mr. Sloan, in seeking to confiscate the interests of

_ the licensed trade without allowing to its members, who
~are deprived of their means of livelihood, the 24 years’
purchase which even some distinguished Nationalist

. landlords have obtained for their land.” We take it

_ that the tip about the 24 years’ purchase is aimed at

' the Chairman of the Perliamentary Party.

Tread on Bung’s toes, and he will tell you what he
thinks of you!  Alderman Hoyne ejaculated—" Send
him back to the Wexford militia.”” Oh, witty Bung!
One Alderman Purcell, a “ Labour ’’ member, seconded
Lenehan’s resolution. Alderman Potter, another Bung,
and a new Jay Pee, with an eye on the Civic Chair,
supported Mr. Bung’s resolution, and praised Mr. Pat
O’Brien, who has been getting some cheap notoriety out
of his beard, or rather his late beard, and the cost of
keeping his chin shaved in the barber’s shop attached to
“the floor.”” Pat O’Brien, it will be remembered, is one
of the great “tried and true’’ patriots who is blocking the
St. Patrick’s Day Closing Bill, and who has ranged him-
self on the side of saint Bung. Mr. Potter thought that
the Kilkenny Corporation ought to call on Mr. O’Mara,
the member for South Kilkenny, not to lend his aid in
support of the Bill. Mr. O’Mara is the patriotic mem-
ber who is attempting to have the St. Patrick’s Day Bill
passed, and he will treat Bung with the consideration he
deserves.  After all, Bung does not own the country
just yet. Alderman Hoyne stated that as “a member
of the aristocracy called Bung, I have a right to add
my few words in support of the proposition.’”” It is
pleasant, anyway, to hear a Mr." Bung branding him-
self with his proper trade mark. Mr. M‘Sweeny
moved :— That we, the members of the Kilkenny Cor-
poration, view with the utmost dissatisfaction the action
of Mr. Pat O’Brien in putting a blocking motion to the
Bill which has for its object the closing of the public-
houses on St. Patrick’s Day, and call on him to with-
draw his opposition to the Bill, and allow it to be passed
into law.”” Mr. Kenealy seconded, and Alderman Cant-
well regretted that the city member blocked the St. Pat-
rick’s Day Bill.

It appears that five-sixths of the public-houses in Kil-
kenny were closed on St. Patrick’s Day, so that fact
gave an opportunity to some subtle Bungs to
trot out fallacies.  And Bung is a neat hand at handling
plausible fallacies in his own interest. Whenever we
hear people saying that Ireland is a  priest-ridden ’’
country, we picture a priest, a Bishop, an Archbishop,
or even a Cardinal arguing with Bung; where the in-
terests of “the trade’ are concerned the theologian,
no matter how expert, would find a surly and tough
dealer in Mr. Bung, “Idolator.”” One Mr. Phelan
asked— Are you going to invoke the British Parlia-
ment to close twenty houses in Kilkenny?’ Well, we
would invoke it; we would invoke the Zulu Parlia-
ment, were there such an institution, to do a like work.
Might we ask Mr. Phelan—‘ Are we not going to invoke
the British Parliament to shut up the vast majority of
Dublin Bungs, including Tallon, Davin, Delahunt,
Mooney, and prevent them from meanly sponging on
St. Patrick?’ One Mr. M‘Carthy asked—* Are you
going to ¢nwoke the British Parliament to make Irish-
men of Irishmen?’ Well, for our part, though it may
appear what Dan Tallon, the keeper of the Magie
Button, might call a “ humiliating reflection ’’ on a sec-
tion of the bipeds inhabiting this country, we would in-
voke anything or any persons under heaven to prevent:
fellows who call themselves Irishmen from sponging on
St. Patrick by pandering for their own unholy profit to
one of the besetting weaknesses of the people of this
country. A Mr. Hogan thought that they would be
only taking the honour from the publicans by passing
the amendment! Oh, they are all honourable men, in:
cluding the Dublin spongers on St. Patrick. :

A Mr. Magennis declared that it would be “a bad
eompliment to Mr. O’Brien to send such a resoluti
from the Kilkenny Corporation—they that stood up so
much for Irish nationality—to dsk England to co:

l:i4

them to celebrate St. Patrick’s Day.”’ Patriotic Mr,
Magennis! The Chairman, a Mr. E. O’Shea, refused
to take the amendment against Mr. Pat O’Brien’s
action with regard to the St. Patrick’s Day closing, and
so Bung’s resolution condemning Mr. William Red-
mond was then “ passed unanimously.”’

At Limerick, Mr. Francis Van Eesbeck, hat and cap
merchant, of George’s Street in that city, was sum-
moned for that he during last July sold a cap as Irish
made that was not Irish made. According to evidence,
the cap was marked inside “Irish made—F. Van
Eesbeck and Co., Hatters, etc., Limerick.”” In the
course of wear, portion of the lining got broken and the
following legend met the naked eye, “ Christy, London,
manufacturer.”” Mr. Gaffney, solicitor, who appeared
for Mr. John Frawley, stated that his client was a
member of the Irish Industrial Association, Limerick,
and he brought the matter before them. Outside Ire-
land a prosecution of that class would be taken up by
the Government or some Government Department where
the Government was in sympathy with the sentiments
of the people of the country, but in Ireland it had to be
taken up by the local body in the city. Quite so. We
have often asked why the Department (Scotch) did not
attempt some useful work of this kind, but you might
as well be whistling jigs to a milestone as asking the
Department (Botch) to do a useful thing of this kind.
Whilst we are over-policed and whilst our policemen,
when they are not receiving testimonials, have little
to do beyond creasing their hair, fishing for eels and
playing concertinas, ‘when a case of this kind arises, it
lies with the purchaser who makes the allegation to go to
the trouble to prosecute. ~ Well, if the Department
(Botch) and the police are no use in a matter of this
kind, the Irish Industrial Association of Limerick took
up the matter. They wrote to Mr. Van Eesbeck, but
Myr. Van Eesbeck did not reply. Eventually a deputa-
tion from the Limerick Industrial Association waited
on the hatter, and the hatter, it is alleged, asked the
members of the deputation why they should expect him
to admit a fraud, and stated that he would never buy
from the traveller again. But he refused to give the
name of the traveller, and he also refused to give a
public apology. The point was raised that the case
should have been brought within six months of the pur-
chase, and, Mr. Hickson the R. M., dismissed the case
according to law but agreed to state a case. We refrain
from comment in the meantime.

The foregoing was written but not published a few
. weeks ago. Since then the case has been tried at Petty
Sessions with the result that the defendant Van Ees-
beck was fined bs. and £1 costs. The defendant
represented that Christy’s traveller—Christy is not a
minstrel but a London hat manufacturer—told Van
Eesbeck that the tweed in the cap was Irish. The
Limerick Industrial Association are to be heartily con-
gratulated for the commendable energy. they showed
in this case. No doubt when Van Eesbeck is selling
another Irish, or reputed Irish, cap his customer will
be anxious for fully convincing evidence. Being a first
offence, Van Eesbeck got off with five shillings and
costs. The next time—but we suppose that Van Ees-
beck is careful now and that there will be no next time.
But what about Christy’s—of London ?

We read that things at one time were burned by the
common hangman. There was a pretty burning at
Sligo recently—a burning by the people. It appears
that recently a Glasgow steamer landed 2} tons of
British reading stuff on the quay at Sligo. This stuff
that we speak of in terms of avoirdupois was to have
been distributed free of charge to the householders of
“obscurant Sligo. Some, at least, of the two tons and a
half was, we are assured, very objectionable to a class of
people which, happily, have to be counted with in these
days—the “ Idolators.”” And the “ Idolators’’ did not
'go and pass an “ unanimous resolution’’ against the
‘typed garbage from Glasgow. Suffering from a fit of
.the verb “to do,”” they went and made a public bonfire

THE LEADER.

'of the lot. Wo hear that further comsignments of

AprIL 14, 1906.

printed garbage, intended for outlying villages, were
countermanded by telegraph. We sympathise with the
people of the county of Sligo who had eagerly looked
forward to more bonfires.

On Sunday week, at the close of a month’s mission by
the Passionist Fathers in Sligo Cathedral, a new Tem-
perance Hall was opened. His Lordship the Bishop of
Elphin presided at the public meeting that was held on
the occasion. The hall, we hear, cost £6,000. It is
well supplied with reading rooms, library, coffee room,
and such things that go to form a thoroughgoing Tem-
perance Club. This class of constructive temperance
effort is badly needed in Ireland. By the way, we
have not yet heard what Mr. Moriarty’s “ All Creeds
All Classes’’ Society or Association is doing with the
money collected at last year’s bazaar. Perhaps some of
our critics with an undue development of “economic
sense ’’ may consider that £6,000 is too much for a poor
town like Sligo to spend on a Temperance Hall!  So long
as Ireland spends thirteen to fourteen millions sterling
on Mr. Bung’s liquors, captious criticism of the expense
of temperance concerns is likely to be unheeded.

An Ennis correspondent assures us that Ennis is the
headquarters of Bung in the South. ~We wonder is
O’Moloney, the Milesian, or Capon, the Cockney, the
commander-in-chief of the occupying army of Bung in
Ireland? He further informs us that in a decaying
town of five thousand persons there are 110 bungeries,
or about one bungery to every forty-five inhabitants.
He complains that notwithstanding this plethora of bun-
geries that Ennis is going down in trade. “I would
say,”” he remarks, “that Ennis is the most gone-down
town in Ireland; it resembles on week days a city of
the dead, with its Saturday weekly markets thinly
peopled and mean of stock, yet the great army of Bung-
men are always alert on the watch, their doors are open,
their guns polished, and ready to give their deadly
shots at a moment’s notice.”” It ought to be clear to
a brilliant imported intellect like that of Mr Capon,
the ex-soldier and Cockney, that what Ennis wants is a
hundred more bungeries.  Emigrants are leaving the
“ Banner County,”” and yet there are only about 110
bungeries in Ennis of 5,000 population. How can we
stop the tide of emigration, Mr. Capon, whilst there is
such comparatively little employment in the Bung line
in the capital of the Banner County?

Mr. Patrick J. Cunningham, 19 Merchant’s Quay,
Dublin, writes us concerning a recent vacancy filled by
the free and patriotic votes of the South Dublin Guar-
dians. The letter induces us to ask again what is the
value of a resolution? Mr. Cunningham writes:—
“ About two months ago the Guardians of the South
Dublin Union passed the following resolution—* That in
future a preference will be given to candidates seeking
positions under the Board who possess a knowledge of
the Irish language.” 1In the daily papers for March@
31st an advertisement appeared announcing a vacancy
for assistant storekeeper to the South Dublin Union. I
quote from the advertisement—‘A preference will be
given to a candidate with knowledge of the Irish lan-
guage.” T became candidate for the position. I lodged
my application, which was written both in English and
in Irish. I canvassed, and the evening before the elec-
tion day sent post-cards written in Irish and in English
to several of the Guardians. On Wednesday, 4th
April, the day of the election, nine candidates, includ-
ing myself, appeared before the Board of Guardians.
The Chairman of the Board asked me if I understood
the Irish language. I replied in the affirmative. The
same question was put to the other candidates, who re-
plied in the negative. There was no language test.. Of
course, we then retired, and and voting was proceeded

with. In the first round of voting I received forty-nine

(49) votes, and in the second round I got only twenty-
eight (28) votes; consequently I was knocked out.”” All
this looks bad in the face of the recent resolution of the
Guardians, and their advertisement.

APRIL 14, 1906.

A correspondent sends us some information under the
title, “ Pagan Belfast '’ :—“To so describe this city of
churches,” he writes, “excluding the pulpits at the
street corners, as also those in the vicinity of the Custom
House, may appear very daring—but does not what
follows justify the designation? In every Christian
land Good Friday, if not recognised as a holiday, is
celebrated as a day of meditation—the most remarkable

- in the calendar, as commemorating the greatest tragedy
that f:ha world has ever witnessed. On that day, of all
days in the year, sports and pastimes are eschewed ; but
not so in Belfast, so noted in the calendar of commer-
cialism for its frauds and gigantic fires and huge failures
—and on the other side, its hypocrisy and hymn-singing.

. What more meet, than that standing out so prominently
in t]{ese respects, Belfast should add to its already well-
acquired distinctions that of having ‘sports’ upon the
day so tragically associated in the annals of Christianity ;
yet such is the case. On the coming Good Friday
‘sports’ are to be held at Celtic Park, which is situated
in the precincts of the historic Falls District—the Catho-
lic and Nationalist section of the city. These  sports’
owe their inception to, and are ‘run’ under, the aus-
pices of the postal employees; aye, the local post office—
and are confined to postal officials, possibly because none
others, by reason of the day upon which they are held,
could be got to join in them—and amongst the competi-
tors that have competed at these sports, which have now
an existence of three or four years, were members of the
Dublin staff, some of whom were, no doubt, ‘ base Idola-
tors.” Had it not been for these helots these ‘sports’
would have hardly had such a lengthy existence This
year they are expected to ‘line up’ in even greater
numbers than on any previous occasion, and thus add to
the laurels they previously obtained at Celtic Park.’”

Our correspondent proceeds : “To secure prizes for thesg
‘sports’ the local officials have been on the cadge solicit-
ing subscriptions for some time. Having got the where-
withal in this respect to obtain trophies, insignificant
somethings to confer upon the successful competitors, at
the ‘ conclusion of the sports on Good Friday ’ it is the
intention of the ‘Sports Committee' to entertain the

‘visiting competitors’ in a local hotel. Tickets
to members of the staff ‘wishing to take part’
in the entertaining, can (so runs the notice)

do so by giving his name, accompanied by 1Is. 6d., to
—well to whom? none other than, as is quite natural
under the circumstances—an ‘Idolator,” one of the spine-
less order, who will ever be found a convenience in such
cases as this. To give his name would be to confer too
great a distinction upon this implement of the fly-catch-
ing fraternity. How can the ‘Idolators’ complain
when those that are of them are the most distinguished
to offer: them insult? What also of those who subscribe
to the fund for the providing of trophies? The premier
subscription comes from the ¢ Idolatrous’ £750 a year
postmaster. What is to be thought of the directorate
‘of the Celtic Park grounds who, on each occasion upon
which these sports have been held, have leased them for
a day for a mere nominal sum—this season it is said £2
—all of whom are ‘ Idolators,” and are of or allied to the
Bung profession? Yet for the sake of ‘tucking in’ a
few pounds they hesitate not to insult their co-reli-
gionists of the premier Catholic district of the city by
Ietting out their grounds for ‘sports’ on a day that
stands out so distinctly by reason of what it commemo-
rates in the Christian calendar. What is most remark-
able in connection with these sports’ is the supineness
of the denizens of the district in which they have been
held.”

It will be inferesting and perhaps surprising to many
people to learn that the granite in the base of the
‘Albert Memorial, Hyde Park, London, was procured
from an Irish quarry. The granite in question, quar-
ried by Messrs. McCartan and Co., at Castlewellan, Co.
Down,. was selected from several competitors for that
important monument. Wae understand that the same
granite was also used in the Eddystone and Blackhead

- lighthouses, and in the coping and caps for Donegal

Road Bridge, Belfast. The Castlewellan granite is of
two colours, grey and blue. We have scen a photograph

* of a handsome Celtic cross of the grey granite recently

THE LEADER. 116

erocted in connection with the memory of ’98. As will
be inferred from the foreging particulars, a consider-
able quantity of this Irish granite is exported every
year, and Messrs. McCartan and Co. also do a big tragde.
in Ireland, though, as is not unusual with such Irish
industries, there is plonty of room for development at
home yet. A local railway extension will, Messrs
McCarban hope, enable them to send more of their
material Dublinwards, where already a sample of
their granite may be found in the steps and landings in
the British Mercantile Assurance Offices, Nassau Street.

A Lisburn correspondent writes:—“In your corres-
pondent’s criticisms of the recent concert in Lisburn
you omit one very important item, viz., that this con-
cert was organised by the local division (405) of the
Ancient Order of Hibernians (Board of Erin). The
local leaders of this organisation, nearly all of whom
are new in matters concerning nationality and perfect
strangers to your ideas of Irish Ireland, are alone re-
sponsible for the disgraceful stuff hashed up here at the
concert. I may also add that said concert has been the
subject of almost universal adverse criticism since,
though it’s only fair to state that the promoters are so
thoroughly ignorant that any criticisms are taken as
purely personal.  All the artistes were strangers to
Lisburn, and I think the great majority will remain so
as both priests and people (with the exceptions men-
tioned) were thoroughly disgusted with them.’””

In the course of a letter dated March 28th, 1906,
Rev. Dr. J. Reddin, Nashua, N.H., U.S.A., writes: —
“You receive so many flattering recommendations that
you may feel quite indifferent to the opinions of a few.
It may give you pleasure, however, to know that you
have a sincere admirer in New Hamp. I heartily sym-
pathize with you in your efforts for the uplifting of my
people at home. Keep them away from this place
where the dollar is God and the servants of Mammon
rule supreme, Thoy have nothing to gain by coming
to America and run great danger of losing the only
thing worth seeking—heaven. May God prosper you in
your efforts, and reward them with abundant fruit.”

A Castlebar correspondent writes informing us that
recently whilst travelling from Dublin ke entered vhe
refreshment room at Mullingar, and asked for a packet
of Irish manufactured cigarettes. He was handed a box
which he took for granted was Irish, but subsequently
he found that the cigarettes were of foreign manufac-
ture. He went back and spoke to the lady assistant.
Eventually she took back the foreign cigarettes, and
returned our correspondent’s money to him. She stated
that they did not stock any Irish cigarettes. :

‘We are rather surprised to find this state of affairs
existing at the present time in connection with the Mid-
land Railway, for we would have expected better busi-
ness since old Cusack was got rid of as Chairman. We
trust that this matter of the boycott of Irish cigarettes
will be raised by Mr. Michael Sullivan at the next half-
yearly meeting of the railway; it indicates a positive
anti-Irish spirit that calls for attack.

Irish cigarettes were, and still are for all we know to
the contrary, boycotted in some or all the refreshment
rooms of the Great Sick and Sourface Railway, and this
notwithstanding that one of the Goodbodys is a director.
Brother Goulding,  gentleman,” took good care that
his lemonade and soda-water were not overlooked, for, as
the public know, the mineral waters of another firm
were displaced from the refreshment rooms of the Great
Sick and Goulding Railway in favour of those of
Goulding’s own make, for Kiloh and Co. are practically
Brother Goulding and family ; they being in fact the de-
velopment of the “ doctor’s shop’’ out of which the
“ gentleman ’’ sprang.

We have received a copy of “ Lectures on Home
Nusing for the Poor,” issued by the Scientific Press,
Ltd., and written by a district nurse. = We have also

source whose authority is of unquestioned weight.

received a favourable opinion as to its merits from a :

We sce that the *usual weekly meeting’ of
Humphrey’s Dump was held at Herbert Park, Mr. W.
M. Murphy, of the Independent and trams, in the chair.
Mr. T. Baker, late of the Independent, we presume, and
Mr. R. S. Tresilian, of the trams, were amongst those
present. Mr. William Anderson, J.P., of the trams,
We presume, was co-opted on to Humphrey’s Dump. The
Scottish Home Industries Association wrote stating it
was their intention to occupy a large space in Hum-
phrey’s Dump. The Campbells are always coming into
this unfortunate country. We suppose this Scotch
Home Industries Show will be the means of dumping
another little cargo of canny Sandys by Liffey’s shoree.
No doubt, Sandy thinks Humphrey’s dump a “verra
gude ’’ move. We trust that the guarantors are putting
by a reserve on deposit in their banks so as to be able to
meet demands promptly.

The following address was presented to the h-officers,
non-coms., and common ‘ Tommies’’ of a British bat-
talion on their leaving Clogheen, in “ Gallant’’ Tip-
perary, for Limerick, where the man Long comes from—
“To the officers, non-commissioned officers and men of
the 70th Battery R.F.A.:—We, the undersigned people
of Clogheen, avail ourselves of the opportunity afforded
to us by your departure from amongst us to convey to
you our lively appreciation of the good-will and esteem
which have existed between the civilians and the officers
and men of the 70th Battery since their arrival here two
years ago. The remembrance of the unfailing courtesy
which the officers and men have ever extended to us, and
their readiness to assist us when organising any form of
amusement, have made a deep and lasting impression on
our minds. We regret the necessities of discipline which
prevents your remaining longer with us, and while we
beg you to accept our ardent wishes for your future pros-
perity, we also sincerely hope that the harmony between
military and civilians which has marked your stay in
Clogheen may long continue uninterrupted.’”’

‘We have not a list of the signatures, but perhaps some
reader would oblige us by sending us one. We hear
that “the Idolatrous’’ as well as the “ Saved ”’ clergy
were represented amongst the signatures, and that
several magistrates, some bank, or bawnk, officials and
many shop-keepers also signed the address. This, in a
-way, is a new departure, as far as our own knowledge
carries, in the address evil. It is bad enough to be pre-
senting addresses to policemen and bank clerks, but
when it comes to presenting addresses from the free and
enlightened ‘“ patriots’’ of Clogheen, in “Gallant’’
Tipperary, to h-officers, non-coms., and common ‘“Tom-
mies ’’ of the British awmy, we may well say “ God Save
Ireland,” especially that part of it called Clogheen, in
*“ Gallant ’’ Tip.

‘We have before us a sort of a booklet concerning a
British flour firm of Cardiff, Bristol, and Newcastle.
Printed matter on the copy before us informs us that
this flour is sold by a Cahirciveen baker. Is it not a
curious thing that Irish flour is not found good enough
for a baker in Catain Sarobin in the extreme West
of Kerry? We fear that railway extension into remote
places like Cahirciveen, whilst it often stimulates imports
-that tend to displace local industries, does not always
provide compensating advantages. The baker who pushes
this flour is a member of the local branch of the Gaelic
League, and we hear that he resigned membership of the
committee because some other member used coal instead
of turf! The Irish milling interests, like the tobacco
manufacturers, are conspicuous by the fight they are
making on behalf of the particular Irish industry with
which they are associated, and it is instructive to notice
what they have to face even from a baker in remote
Cahirciveen—a baker, too, who is a member of the
Gaelic League, and particularly objects to others using
- British coal rather than Irish turf. A cynic might sug-
* gest that possibly he has a turf-bog himself.

An Anti-Testimonial and Address: League is badly
anted. Some great nation-making performances in
shape of the Moy Valley Races came off recently in
South of Carlow county.. And they could not get

THE LEADER.

APRIL 14, 1906.

up this ninepence-ha’penny race mcgbi.ng but someone
should be presented with an address in connection with
it! A Mr. Charles Butler, whose chief social distinction
as far as wo know is that he is one of the “Saved”” to
whom the Seonin “Idolators’’ naturally look aloft,
was waited upon by a deputation, and the deputation
laid an address at his feet. Had not the members of
this deputation, whoever they were, anything better
to do with their time?

By all accounts the mud-throwers of the so-called Uni-
versity in College Green had not it all their own way
during the Lord Mayor’s procession. We heu'-‘ that
some young street; boys lined the railings of the Un_;-
versity ”” of spelling fame, and harangued the mud-
throwers in this fashion:—Spell Mayor—m-a-I-e—
Mayor—go in and write that down before you forget
it.””  Weo certainly think that though some of the
Tyinity College “gentlemen’’ may have landed rotten
eggs in the Lord Mayor's carriago, thab the street
urchins had the better of it in the matter of wit.

We have not yet received a list of the Nutting

medium. We wonder which is the bigger humbug—
Alf Foxz or George?

Dear Sir,—I regret that I cannot reply separately te
all who have been so heartily promising practical sup-
port for the proposed Irish Mutual Insurance Company,
but, when the totals of the business, thus promised,
reach a figure at which the new scheme may safely be
launched, every promised supporter shall be communi-
cated with and requested to attend a meeting in Dublin
for the purpose of electing Trustees and considering the
bye-laws (which, I might state, are already compiled)
to govern the Company. I am sure that every patriotie
Trish man and woman is in favour of the projected en-
terprise, and I trust that those of your readers who
have not yet written to me, as suggested in the LEADER
of 3rd March, will do so within the next few weeks.—
Yours truly,

AN INsURANOR MANAGER.

Sir,—It is, indeed, very gratifying to learn from the
able and well-reasoned article of “ Insurance Manager *’
in a recent LeApmr, that so much support in favour
of the new Irish company is forthcoming. But, then,
why not it be supported, and well supported too, by
everybody in the country.

If the people would only think a bit, and reason the
thing out for themselves, would it not be clear to them

that it is their own interest they would be supporting,

since it is the policy-holders, under the proposed system,
would be the owners of the comoany and the sole gainers
therehy. . : B

. Are we not at present in Ireland supporting English,

Dear Sir,—Some of the city papers on Friday and
Saturday week published reference to the case of Van-
ner and Prest ». us, which was decided recently in the
Chancery Court, London.  As probably the matter
might lead to some notice by you if you saw the reports
—the case being somewhat unique, we keg to supplement
the meagre report which appeared by the following
facts : —

Last Spring we registered the “ Shamrock,’’ name and
device as our Trade Mark for polishes of all kinds.
Some months afterwards we discovered that Messrs.
Vanner and Prest, of London, were importing a “ Sham-
rock ’ boot cream. We drew their attention to the fact
that they were infringing our rights, but they claimed
prior use and called upon us to have the entire mark ex-
punged. We of course refused to do anything of the
kind, and they applied to the High Court for an order
that our mark be expunged from the Register.

We were advised that they could successfully dispute
our exclusive right to the mark in respect of their own
special article on-the ground of prior use, and the case
was perforce settled on these ‘lines, we paying costs.
The effect is that our mark is to remain on the Register
subject to an amendment, which will give them a right
to the name in common with us for the article on which
they used it, eo that they now have a free hand in
sending foreign stuff under the “ Shamrock > brand.

The Dublin papers gave an extremely brief notice
and made no comment whatever. The Freeman did not
even insert the report.. Excuse us for troubling you
for such length.—We are, yours truly,

INDUSTRIAL REVIVAL AND THE PRODUCERS.

The report of the Shareholders’ Committee of the
Killaloe Slate Co., Ltd., is interesting, if not encourag-
ing, reading. In the course of the report we read:—
« It is regrettable that for many years no provision was
made for the renewal of machinery by writing off a
sufficient sum for depreciation. Every time a dividend
could be snatched it was taken, and no provision made
for the future.”” That is a pretty commentary on the
directors of this unfortunate concern. Mr. Oliver Fry,
J.P., one of the directors, presided at the adjourned
meeting. He stated that they were deeply indebted to
the Committee for their very valuable report and recom-
mendations; and Mr. Tuthill, the director who re-
minded Mr. R. P. Gill that though he (Mr. Tuthill)
was a country gentleman he had been a gold medalist
in College, and that it, therefore, could hardly be said
that he was perfectly incompetent to deal with figures—
Mr. Tuthill was at the adjourned meeting and said the
report was most valuable! Poor old Directors. Here
is another extract from this most valuable shareholders’
report :—“ The boiler is in use thirty years, and has
never been insured, and no regular inspection of it has
ever been carried out. The waggons are wretched old
affairs, in very bad condition, and give the very worst
results for the power expended on them, both hand and
steam. All the haulage machinery is very bad, and
indeed worthless, and causes enormous waste of time and
money.”’  What is the use of putting a gold medalist
on a Board of Directors if he will not see after the
machinery, and see that the boiler is insured? The
conclusion’ of this most valuable shareholders’ report is
rather severe on the management, in fact it is like a
great mass of overhanging rock falling on the manage-
ment, gold medalist and all. It runs as follows:—
“ The management should be such as would correct
steadily the faults at present existing both as to the
economic application of the labour and the business-like
and rapid carrying on of sales. This management is
non-existent, at present.’’

Mr. 'I:‘uthill, the gold medallist, trotted out a list of
the various dividends paid since 1893 when he joined

the Board. He boasted that in 20 successive half-years

£11,974 11s. 5d. was paid in dividends. "During the

time, or part of the time, that this, roundly, £12,000 .

was being paid, “no provision was made for the re-
newal of machinery by writing off a sufficient sum for

 depreciation ;* and during all this time the boiler was
uninsured and no regular inspection of it was carried
out, and to-day “all the haulage machinery is very
bad, and indeed worthless.”” What sort of business
men can the directors have been? The Shareholders’
Committee recommend now that the wholo plant be
cast, and ‘that Proper waggons, buckets, and all ap-
pliances of a modern and economic class should be
supplied. And the Chairman of the Company that did
not insure its boiler, or make provision for renewal of
m'a(_:hmery,_ though about £12,000 was paid out in
dividends in ten years, said at the adjourned meeting
that he “thought they all agreed that they were
deeply indebted to the Committee for their very valuable
report and recommendations, and, speaking for the
boa,rfl, they would endeavour to carry out practically the
details in the fullest way, but, of course, they had the
great difficulty about capital and finance to deal with,
which had been the drawback up to the present.”” How
could they have capital if they were paying dividends
out of capital, for surely a company that distributes
its “ earnings ** whilst it makes no provision for renewal
of machinery, is paying its dividends at least partly out
of capital. Well, if there was no regular inspection of
the boiler, it was none too soon that the management
was overhauled.

The Dark Brothers of Killaloe who did not for thirty
years insure their boiler, and who made no provisioa
for renewal of machinery, have over £1,500 worth of
stock in hand, and the shareholders’ report points out
that “ no effort is being made to sell this stock by adver-
tising or canvassing.”” Every one who wants a slate,
we suppose, ought to take train to Killaloe to buy it,
and perhaps when he arrives there pay an extra 25 per
cent. for it because it was one of an unadvertised stock
held in the Killaloe cave! We have done, and are

- doing, our part, and an effective part it was, and is, in

stirring up the consumer, but no disposition on the
part of the consumer can bring about a satisfactory in-
dustrial state of affairs so long as producers play their
side of the game in the slip-shod and rather unbusiness-
like manner that the Shareholders’ Committee of the
Killaloe Slate Co., have laid bare before the public.
Business management like that disclosed in this case is
calculated to create a reaction against Irish industry,
and that falls very hard on those many producers who
are making adequate efforts to do their part of the work
in a thorough-going manner.

Mr. R. P. Gill and those associated with him, who de-
manded this Shareholders’ Committee, deserve well of
the country at large. Their action showed what can
be done, and also indicates what may be found out,
when it is done. The shareholders of every unsatis-
factory public company in the country would do well to
consider the advisability of pressing for a Shareholders”
Committee of Inquiry. Directors who don’t even in-
sure their Company’s boiler should not be allowed to
clog the wheels of Irish industrial progress. There 13
a certain sort of Ascendancy in higher business circles
in Ireland, and a good many old fogies have a com-
mercial reputation which they do not deserve. The
awful collapse of the “ National > Assurance Company
was one of the first eye-openers that the people of this
country got in recent years regarding the business value
of some ‘‘ commercial magnates.”’ We hope that the
shareholders of Brother Goulding’s Railway will keep
pegging away for a Shareholders’ Committee until they
get it. We would be interested to know if Brother
Goulding’s Company’s boilers are insured, and if ade-
quate provision has been made for remewal of
machinery.

LEADER. 119

Examiner: It will help them to understand how the

This week a most representative Munster deputation
travelled to London to interview the Premier about the
-Canadian Cattle Question. Next morning Southern
readers anxiously scanned their Ezaminer’s enlightening
leading articles to sece what resulted from the interview.
Fancy our intense astonishment when we read :—

“Sir Henry assured the deputation of his personal
sympathy, but he warned them it would be no easy
business to get this Act of Parliament repealed, that
they were very much mistaken if they thought so, and
that they should not pitch their hopes too high, as they
had undoubtedly got Ireland and a great part of Eng-
land against them.”’

There was a slap in the face for the deputation! Of
course your readers know that Sir Henry said no such
thing—this was pant of a speech he made to a ScorcH
deputation some weeks ago!!

It is in the “ London Correspondence’’ I find on
March 26th—“T understand that the health of Mr. Joe
Chamberlain is again causing grave anziety,”” and on
March 27th (next day) I read in an account of a-meeting
where Joe attended, “Mr. Chamberlain, who is in
excellent health!”

The London correspondent is a dreadful prophet. He
knows things—he does. Read this: “T am loth to make
sensational statements, but from information I have re-
ceived there can be no doubt about the German Em-
peror’s intention to bring about a war with France
& o Therefore the outbreak may take place at
any moment.’’

He spends all his time with Continental Crowned
Heads when he is not with the Royal family. The
value of his statements, however, may be judged from
a letter appearing to-day from a firm in which they
state—“ Two paragraphs appeared recently in your
paper under the heading  London Correspondence,’ to
the effect’’—the letter is too long to quote, but the
writer ends with “ The paragraph is absolutely without
foundation !’

600 people to Cork, and the Balls and Social reunions
held over night to usher in the festival, lent to dalliance
within doors after being overnight at Capua!”’ And
the report ends with this stimulating passage:—“In
the change that has come one can see clearly, improve-
ment, and progress, where, freed from being ‘ braced
and brazened up with Christmas wines,” our people can
pass their holidays, as the other days that are to them,
in the enjoyment of that woudrous and intellectual
imagination with which Providence has endowed the
Celtic race, and which needs no stumulus but what
reason and nature brings to check its exuberance.’”’
This is “ Institute of Journalists >’ English!

after expressing himself “ strongly in favour of the Con- -
ference which Mr. O’Brien—"’ wound up with this
thrilling sentence:—* He said those who would refuse
that Conference would undertake a serious responsi-
bility, which the labourers of Ireland and the workers
of Ireland, through their organised movement, would
remand an explanation of the Members of the Irish
Parliamentary Party should be on platforms of that
kind, voicing the demands of the labourers (cheers).””

have had the book in our office for a very long time ’’
(ibid., p. 82). He thus showed that he was acquainted
with the Deed, and that he knew it was in the Minute
Book. And knowing it was in the Minute Book, and in
the custody of the Secretary of his Commission, he
asked Mr. Wright, K.C., Counsel for the Erasmus Smith
Board, to send him a copy of it! He thus avoided all
personal responsibility for the document which he has
quoted in his judgment (p. 244); he quoted Mr.
Wright's version, and also made it evidence, by taking
it from the custody of the Board of Erasmus Smith.
He had already given it as the opinion of himself and
Mr. Justice O’Brien, that any documents in the cus-
tody of the Board were evidence.

All this romancing of Dr. Dodd, of Lord Justice
Fitzgibbon, and their friends about the private reli-
gious opinions of Erasmus Smith—if he had any—is
beside the question. For Tudor (p. 116) has laid it
down as Chancery law, that “in the case of an eleemosy-
nary charity ’—such as Erasmus Smith’s is— o o
“all evidence as to the peculiar tenets and opinions of
the founder is inadmissible, as evidence of his inten-
tion '

Lord Justice Fitzgibbon has said that Puritanism,
or anti-Episcopalianism, and Episcopalianism, are two
sections of the one Common Protestantism, and that
therefore notwithstanding the change from anti-Epis-
copalianism to Episcopalianism, the Endowment ve-
mained Protestant.

I have already given two sufficient replies to this
argument; but even if the Lord Justice’s contention
were right, it would take him no further. For even
if Puritanism and Episcopalianism were sections of the
same common Protestantism, as Erasmus Smith was

compelled by the laws of Cromwell’s day to impose
Puritanism on his Endowment, and was compelled by
the laws of Charles the Second’s day to impose Epis-
copalianism on it, he was compelled by the laws of the
days of Cromwell and Charles II. to impose Puri-
tanism plus Episcopalianism, or Protestantism, on it;
and, therefore, according to the Lord Justice’s own
canon of evidence, the imposition of Protestantism on
the Endowment is no evidence of Protestant intentions
in Erasmus Smith. And henco when Lord Justice Fitz-
gibbon says in his judicial decision (p 244):—“T1f T am
right that Protestantism is of the esseuce of the
Founder’s intention, the scheme of the Rev. Mr. Hum-
phrys, of course, falls to the ground;’’ he is not right;
Protestantism is not of the essence of the Founder’s in-
tention, but of the essence of the penal laws of Crom-
well and of Charles IT., now repealed; and the scheme
of the Rev. Mr. Humphrys holds the field, whilst the
fantastic house of cards, built so ingeniously by the
Lord Justice on a false foundation, tuwbles to the
ground. If the Lord Justice were right, the intention
and trust of Erasmus Smith would be fulfilled by giving
secular education and Protestant instruction to the
Hindoos. He omits altogether the all-important ques-
tiocn—" What is the class of persons intended to be
benefited ?”’ Who are the beneficiaries? Which Tudor
tells us (p. 146) “is, in every case, a question of the
Founder’s intention.”” Tudor also tells us (:bid.) that
it is one of the most important principles in Chancery
law. For he gives it as one of the two principles for
the application of the Cy-pres doctrine, “ that the bene-
fit of the charity must not be extended beyond the class
of persons for whom the donor intended his bounty to
be applied.”” The other principle is, “that the object
selected must be the nearest that can be found to that
which has failed.”” It is, therefore, a settled, funda-
mental principle of Chancery law, that even where the
Cy-prés doctrine is to be applied the Endowment cannot
be taken from the beneficiaries. How much more,
then, is it a fundamental principle when the original
trusts can be carried into effect? D. H.

AN IRISH SONG BOOK.*

LL lovers of Irish songs, as well as the young
folks in the schools, will give a hearty welcome

to Part IT1. of the Cnuapaco, the first two parts of
which have become so deservedly popular. Part III.,
like its predecessors, is entirely in Irish, the musical
notation being the tonic sol-fa. A complete vocabu-
lary ig provided of all the new words occurring over
and above those to be found in the vocabularies of
the two previous parts. It confains sixteen songs,
several of which have been published now for the first
time. The genuineness of the Trish verses is suffi-
ciently guaranteed by the names of such well-known
writers of Irish as Riobiyo Vetoon (Robert Weldon),
the “ Comeragh Poet,” “To6mna’’ (Tavg Ua ‘Oonn-
cav4), “Paopaic” (P. O Dpom), and Opbopn O
haunhipsin (Osborne Bergin), over which they appear.
The airs to which the words are adapted ave all old-
time melodies of haunting beauty, most of which
have been taken down from living traditional singers
of the old tunes. That an air is distinctly old Irish
is not sufficient to justify its being inserted in a book
of songs intended for children. I'ather Walshe has

not overlooked this point, and most of the songs in
Part III. are of a sufficiently sprightly nature to
aftract the attention of young ears, and to make these
songs life-long favourites. ~Amongst them is to be
found that fine air ““Billy Byrne of Ballymanus,”
which has been made so popular by Mr. Ludwig, and
to which words by Topna have been adapted. An
entirely new and very pretty version is given of the
air ““Caitin ear cparote na m-v6,” as learnt by An
T-Atain O Riogdin from the playing of a fiddler near
Mitchelstown.  The latter half of the tune is parti-
cularly beautiful, being in strong conftrast with the
simple plaintiveness of the first half, expressive of
sanguine hope. Many Gaelic Leaguers will be glad
to have the traditional air and words of ¢ Capat an
Tragdin,” as sung by a prize-winner in the féip na
Muman.

But, I suppose, there is nothing perfec
the sun.

It is questionable whether it was wise to attempt to
dissociate the “Mo thaipe”’ of “Paopaic’” from the air
by which Mr. O’'Mara has popularised it. The air of
““The Shamrock” seems to suit it better. Besides,
in adapting it to the new air the rollicking chorus has
had to be omitted.

It is doubtful whether we ought to approve of
inserting arbitrary notes in the musical text here and
there, by repeating the previous note so as to supply
for extra syllables in the works. This practice
may lead to corruption of airs or somewhat alter their
character by the changing of the rhythm of the
melody which if necessitates. In any case, it is
confusing to have so many notes bracketed to be sung
only in certain verses. It must be particularly con-
fusing to school children.

One other fault must be found with what is other-
wise an admirable publication. There are a couple
of cases ocecurring in which notes which are an
essential part of the melody are left without any
syllable or word to which fo be sung. In the tenth
song—“ODuan na Ssompe "-—the very first note
s which opens the melody is left out in three out of
the four verses. It can be found that the first syllable
of the three verses in question can be also adapted to
the s;. The same defect is noticeable in the first
verse of the thirteenth song, ‘‘Capad an Tragdn,”
and apparently without reason.
ight defects do not much defract from this
otherwise excellent little song book. Father Walshe,
the indefatigable compiler of the Cnuapa¢o, has
earned the gratitude of Irish Ireland for this further
contribution to Irish minstrelsy, and wishing a God-
speed and a good sale to Part IIL, we Lope soon to
be able to give another welcome to Part IV. of the
Cnuapraco. Fean 6 Copcas.

NOTIOE in your issue of the 7th inst. a letter from 
" An Old Kerry Inhabitant " pointing out a-11 
omission in my article on T'.ralee published 17th ult. 
The omission he refers tQ is an important one, and I en-
tirely agree with all that he says. It was quite an over-
sight on my part to omit the name of the firm of Messrs. 
T. Galvin and Son from among the names -of leading 
merchants who have made Tralee the prosperous busi-
ness :town it is, and the firm deserves special mention for 
its branch of business called the Kerry Knitting Fac-
tory-a-n industry which employs more girls than any es-
tablishment in the town .. For -the benefit of your readers 
who.may not know by repute the merits of this impor-
tant Ken-y industry, I would refer them to your adver-
tisement columns where the name of the Kerry Knitting 
Co. appears. 
I can also, from my own'' experience, 
highly recommend the quality of their goods. 
While I a-m glad of .this opportunity of doing justice 
to' a firm whi-0h has quietly and unostentaitiously done 
good to the people of Tralee for many years, I would 
wish that some other Kerry inhabitant, old or young, 
would tell us what might be done to arouse an interest 
in the Irish La.nguage . Movement among the .general 
population of Tralee-especially the . well-to-do cla.ss. 
It will be remembered that in my article of the 17th ult. 
I said:-" I wrote--not to show what a prosperous town 
Tra.Iee was from a business point of view-but to see if 
any remarks of mine would have any influence in pro-
moting an interest in the Irish Language Movement, 
which is considered of such little consequence in the 
town." Nobody has demurred as to my remarks on the 
apathy towards the language question, therefore I take 
it nobody cares. 
' 
I suppose the fact is, that the people of T.ralee are too 
much ta.ken up with business matters to trouble about 
the Irish Langu-age-a subscription they will give, when 
asked, but they do not want to be troubled any further 
-this peing .so, it has occurred to me t~at the merchants 
of the town might be got to take an interest through 
the industrial movement. 
I would propose -that the 
Tl'alee Feis of 1907 should be a county Feis to occupy 
two days; one for Irish Language, e.tc., Go:mpetitions, 
and the second day for Industrial Exhibits from all 
places within the county. 
'.Ifhere is plenty time to work 
out the idea in detail, and the fact could' be well adver-
tised during the year. 
The merchants of Tralee are at 
any .rate capable and enterprising business men, and 
we could'expect a fine turn-out from the industrial side 
under the supervision of a committee composed of the 
merchants, while the interests of the language could be 
looked to by the local branch of the Gaelic League-this 
blending of language and industrial interests too-ether 
could not fail to have a good effect in time. 
0 
As the Gaelic League propaganda has been now some 
years in ope-ration in Kerry,_ it would be weU to have a 
county Feis, so that all our young talented native 
speakers and learners should be known and appreciated. 
I hope the proposal of a county Feis for next year will 
be warmly taken up by both the Gaelic League and mer-
chants of 'Ira.lee, and if it is, I feel sure it will turn out 
to be an unqualified success. .
